coredata.dc:title: Coupling single-atom Cu and N-enriched π-conjugated carbon nanodots on graphene enables room-temperature ppb-level DMMP detection

coredata.dc:description: The graphene-based room-temperature chemiresistive gas sensors have low sensitivity (especially for ppb-level organophosphorous compounds, OPCs), limiting their practical applications. Herein, a novel coupling single-atom Cu and N-enriched, π-conjugated carbon nanodots (NCDs) strategy is proposed to synthesize graphene-based sensing materials with excellent room-temperature DMMP sensing performances. Taking rGO-NCDs-Cu as the example, the N-enriched structures facilitate the adsorption of DMMP by providing more active sites and strengthening the hydrogen bonding interactions between -NH2 and DMMP molecules. The π-conjugated structures can construct large π-delocalization network promoting charge generation and transport. The N-enriched and π-conjugated structures also result in narrowing band gap of NCDs, effectively enabling the increase of carrier transfer rate. Moreover, the introduction of single-atom Cu sites with Cu-N2Cl2 structure further strengthens the hydrogen bonding interactions between sensing materials and DMMP molecules. Benefiting from this novel coupling strategy, rGO-NCDs-Cu sensor shows high response value of 13.95 % towards 340 ppb DMMP at room temperature. Meanwhile, rGO-NCDs-Cu sensor exhibits short response time (77 s) and recovery time (198 s), low practical limit of detection (34 ppb), excellent repeatability and high selectivity. Our work offers a new strategy for rational design of graphene-based materials for detection of OPCs at room temperature.

(a) Schematic illustration of chemical reactions for the synthesis of NCDs from oPD, (b) XRD pattern, (c) AFM image, (d) elemental analysis results, (e) N 1 s spectrum, (f) UV-Vis absorption spectrum and fluorescent emission spectrum of NCDs (excitation at 432 nm) and (g) the photographs of NCDs dispersion under UV light.

(a) A scheme of material structure, (b) N 1 s spectrum, (c) N species and contents, (d) SEM image of rGO-NCDs. (e) The energy band positions of rGO and NCDs and energy band diagram of rGO-NCDs heterojunction. (f) A scheme of the material structure, (g) FT-IR spectrum and (h) high-resolution TEM image of rGO-NCDs-Cu.

(a) Cu 2p XPS spectrum, (b) Cu species and contents of rGO-NCDs-Cu. (c) XANES spectra and (d) the magnification of the pre-peak region of Cu k-edge of rGO-NCDs-Cu, Cu foil, CuPc, CuO and Cu2O, (e) the mean chemical valences of Cu species in rGO-NCDs-Cu, (f) EXAFS spectra of Cu k-edge, (g) the corresponding EXAFS fitting curves of rGO-NCDs-Cu at R space.

(f) Schematic illustration of sensing process for rGO-NCDs-Cu-based DMMP sensor. (g) Comparisons of the present rGO-NCDs-Cu sensor with other room-temperature DMMP sensors in previous publications.

head.ce:title: Coupling single-atom Cu and N-enriched π-conjugated carbon nanodots on graphene enables room-temperature ppb-level DMMP detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The graphene-based room-temperature chemiresistive gas sensors have low sensitivity (especially for ppb-level organophosphorous compounds, OPCs), limiting their practical applications. Herein, a novel coupling single-atom Cu and N-enriched, π-conjugated carbon nanodots (NCDs) strategy is proposed to synthesize graphene-based sensing materials with excellent room-temperature DMMP sensing performances. Taking rGO-NCDs-Cu as the example, the N-enriched structures facilitate the adsorption of DMMP by providing more active sites and strengthening the hydrogen bonding interactions between -NH2 and DMMP molecules. The π-conjugated structures can construct large π-delocalization network promoting charge generation and transport. The N-enriched and π-conjugated structures also result in narrowing band gap of NCDs, effectively enabling the increase of carrier transfer rate. Moreover, the introduction of single-atom Cu sites with Cu-N2Cl2 structure further strengthens the hydrogen bonding interactions between sensing materials and DMMP molecules. Benefiting from this novel coupling strategy, rGO-NCDs-Cu sensor shows high response value of 13.95 % towards 340 ppb DMMP at room temperature. Meanwhile, rGO-NCDs-Cu sensor exhibits short response time (77 s) and recovery time (198 s), low practical limit of detection (34 ppb), excellent repeatability and high selectivity. Our work offers a new strategy for rational design of graphene-based materials for detection of OPCs at room temperature.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Benefiting from the merits of high efficacy and low cost, organophosphorous pesticides (OPs) have been extensively used in agriculture, playing an important role in crop protection and insect eradication [1,2]. Unfortunately, residual OPs in the gas and water environments could enter the human body through skin, respiratory system and interact with acetylcholinesterase to produce stable compounds, leading to acute toxic poisoning and disorders related to human nerve function [3,4].

In order to protect ecosystems and human health, there is an urgent need to monitor concentrations of residual OPs in environment. In the past decades, several conventional approaches have primarily been employed to analyze quantitatively liquid phase OPs, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), immunoassays and colorimetric method [5]. However, the complex pretreatment process limits their practical applications, especially for OPs detection in atmospheric environment. Owing to the volatility of OPs, gas phase detection technique is capable to assess OPs concentration in air. Fortunately, the fast-developing gas sensor technology has satisfied the demand for a speed, cost-effective and reliable approach for analysis of OPs [6,7]. Typically, gas sensors, as small analytical devices containing gas recognition elements and signal transducers, become the promising candidate for in-situ detection of gaseous OPs, possibly achieving good sensitivity and specificity [8]. Due to their extraordinarily high toxicity, OPs are limited in normal laboratories for safety concerns. To overcome this limitation, DMMP was selected as a typical low-toxicity simulant for OPs. Therefore, development of gas sensors for ppb-level DMMP detection is crucial for environmental protection and human safety [9].

To date, a series of gas sensors with different principles have been successfully developed for room-temperature DMMP detection. For example, several mass-sensitive sensors operated by analyzing mass changes of the devices during the adsorption/desorption of DMMP molecules were constructed, which are dependent on unique principles, including surface acoustic wave (SAW) [10], quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) [11] and resonant microcantilever [12]. Moreover, other DMMP sensors have been fabricated by conversing DMMP concentration into electrical signals [13–15]. Although these DMMP sensors exhibit fast response rate and high sensitivity, their further practical applications are limited by the complex device structure and exorbitant price. Alternatively, chemiresistive gas sensors have been widely investigated due to their advantages of low cost, simple device structure, stable signal output, currently considered as widespread and productive gas sensors [16]. Therefore, the development of chemiresistive DMMP sensors has important research significance. Recently, a bioelectronic nose platform for DMMP detection was constructed by Ni2 + ions-decorated reduced graphene oxide (Ni-rGO) modified with human olfactory receptor hOR2T7, realizing the detection of 1 ppb-1000 ppm DMMP in N2 [17]. However, this novel sensing platform shows the obvious disadvantages of complex bioengineering for preparation of hOR2T7, poor stability and unapplicable to detect DMMP in air. Inspired by forming hydrogen bonds between sensing materials and DMMP, we have recently designed room-temperature DMMP sensors based on PPy-modified rGO (PPy-rGO) hybrids, exhibiting reversible response for 5–100 ppm DMMP [18]. Unfortunately, it cannot meet the ppb-level DMMP detection. Therefore, it is critical to regulate the hydrogen bonding strength for constructing DMMP sensors for ppb-level DMMP detection.

Experimentally, DMMP molecules can be specifically adsorbed on materials surface at room temperature through hydrogen bonds between electron-rich O atoms in P = O bonds of DMMP and electron-deficient H atoms in N-H bonds of materials [19]. That is to say, DMMP is a typical strong electron donor and transfers electrons to the acceptor N-H by forming a hydrogen bond bridge. Therefore, the electron cloud density in acceptor N-H directly affects the hydrogen bond strength. From the view of regulating electron cloud density, several strategies have been developed to regulate hydrogen bonding strength. For example, improvement of DMMP sensing performances was observed by grafting HFIP onto Co3O4/rGO, owing to enhancing hydrogen bonding strength by regulating the electron cloud density of O-H with trifluoromethyl groups [20]. Similarly, conjugated organic polymers containing B-N units were designed for NH3 detection, where introduction of B atoms leads to regulating the electron cloud density of N-H, enhancing the ability to recognize NH3 through hydrogen bonding [21]. Recently, we have reported the improvement of DMMP sensing performances for rGO-based room-temperature DMMP sensors through strengthening hydrogen bonding interactions by modification of HFIP [19] and decoration with metal ions [22]. However, the available strategies for introducing specific functional groups and atoms still suffer from complex synthetic methods and high cost for preparation.

Alternatively, carbon nanodots (CDs) are environmentally friendly and easily prepared zero-dimensional nanomaterials that have shown great potential for gas sensing applications in recent years owing to their excellent optical/electrical properties, high chemical/thermal stability, and tunable surface states [23–27]. Inspired by these thoughts, to achieve ppb-level DMMP detection for graphene-based DMMP sensors, herein, a coupling single-atom Cu and N-enriched, π-conjugated structures was proposed to improve room-temperature DMMP sensing performances, benefiting from the simultaneous regulation of hydrogen bonding interactions and semiconductor properties. Obviously, the N-enriched, π-conjugated CDs (NCDs) with yellow fluorescence obtained from o-phenylenediamine (oPD) were firstly used to modify rGO. The synergy of N-enriched structure, π-conjugated structure and narrowed band gap results in significantly improving DMMP sensing performances.

As a proof of concept, a novel ternary graphene-based sensing material of NCDs and single-atom Cu co-decorated rGO was rationally designed (rGO-NCDs-Cu). As expected, rGO-NCDs-Cu sensor exhibits excellent room-temperature DMMP sensing performances, including high response (13.95 % to 340 ppb DMMP), low limit of detection (34 ppb), short response time (77 s) and short recovery time (198 s), Finally, the reason for enhanced DMMP sensing performances of rGO-NCDs-Cu sensor is also deeply investigated.

The oPD was purchased from Aike Reagent. NaNO3, citric acid (CA), H2O2 (30 wt%) and CuCl2·2 H2O were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. KMnO4 was obtained from Beijing Chemical Corp. Graphite, urea and ascorbic acid (AA) were obtained from Aladdin Ltd. All chemicals were used as received without further purification. The water used throughout all experiments was purified through a Millipore system.
GO was prepared by a modified Hummers’ method [28].

In a typical synthesis of rGO-NCDs, 0.5 mL of GO dispersion (1.0 mg/mL) and 4.5 mL of oPD solution (1 mg/mL) were added into 15.5 mL of H2O. After sonication for 10 min, the dispersion was irradiated by UV light (300 W) for 30 min at room temperature. Then, the mixture was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 ºC for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solid products were collected and washed with water several times, followed by dryness at 60 ºC for 24 h.

The rGO-NCDs-Cu was prepared by adsorption of Cu2+ ions by rGO-NCDs. Briefly, 2.0 mg of as-prepared rGO-NCDs was dispersed into 10 mL of H2O. After sonication for 10 min, 10 mL of CuCl2 solution containing 170 mg of CuCl2·2 H2O was added to the aforesaid dispersion (Cu2+ ions concentration in the mixture is 50 mM). After stirring for 2 h at room temperature, the rGO-NCDs-Cu was obtained by centrifugation and washing with water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of sensors and gas sensing measurements
ce:section.ce:para: Dropping-coasting technology was used to construct DMMP sensors. The ceramic plate with a thickness of 0.6 mm, a length of 7 mm, and a width of 5 mm printed with graphitic interdigitated electrodes (3 pairs) was used as substrate. The sensing film was constructed by dropping 7.5 μL of aqueous dispersion of various sensing materials onto ceramic substrate. The prepared gas sensors were subjected to heat treatment with infrared fast dryer for 20 min, and then aging treatment kept at 100℃ for 4 h.

The desired DMMP with different concentrations was obtained by controlling the flow rate of dry air and standard DMMP gas (3.4 ppm) by mass flow meters (the total flow rate was 1000 mL/min). The entire gas sensing measurements were performed at nearly 0 % relative humidity (RH) and 25 ºC. NO2 and NH3 were prepared by injecting a certain volume of standard target gas (1 % NO2 in N2, 1 % NH3 in N2) into the test chamber. Ethanol and methanol were prepared by injecting a certain volume of ethanol liquid (99.5 wt%) and methanol liquid (99.7 wt%) into the test chamber. The sensor response is defined as S (%) = (R g-R a)/R a× 100 % for reducing gas, and S (%) = (R a-R g)/R a× 100 % for oxidizing gas, where R a and R g are the resistances of the sensor exposed to air and target gas.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were recorded on a RigakuD/MAX 2550 diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed on an ESCALABMK II X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometer (ICP-AES) was performed on Agilent 5110. X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) measurements were conducted at BL14W1 in the Shanghai Synchrotron Radiation Facility (SSRF). The storage ring of SSRF was working at the energy of 3.5 GeV under “top-up” mode with a constant current of 260 mA. UV spectroscopy (UV-2450), fluorescence spectrometer (RF-5301 PC) and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, WQF-510A) were used to assess the spectral data. Field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, JSM-6700 F) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, JSM-2100 F) were used to examine the morphology and microstructure of the materials. CHI-660D electrochemical analyzer (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd.) was used to generate Mott-Schottky curves. The atomic force microscope (AFM, Dimension FastScan) was used to analyze the morphology of NCDs. The gas sensing properties were examined using a CGS-8 intelligent test meter (Beijing Elite Tech. Co., Ltd., China).

For traditional metal oxides-based gas sensors operated at high temperature, realization of gas sensing is majorly depended on the redox reactions between target gas and oxygen species on metal oxides [29,30]. By contrast, the determining of DMMP at room temperature is generally depended on the charges transferring between DMMP molecules and sensing materials. Therefore, the rational design of a suitable sensitive material containing an electron transfer bridge with high transferring efficiency between DMMP and sensing materials is a promising approach to improve the DMMP sensing performances. Herein, we proposed a coupling single-atom Cu and N-enriched, π-conjugated NCDs strategy to design sensing material and construct room-temperature DMMP sensors. As shown in Fig. 1a, the DMMP sensor consists of a bottom ceramic substrate, a middle carbon interdigitated electrode and a top sensitive material film. The sensitive material is composed of three components: rGO, NCDs and single-atoms Cu. The principles for design of this sensing material are proposed as following three aspects. (I) Increasing the adsorption ability of DMMP by introduction of N-enriched structures in NCDs. The increase of N content in NCDs not only could form more N-H bonds for direct adsorption of DMMP, but also decreases electronic cloud density of N atom in N-H bonds, thereby strengthening the hydrogen bonding interactions. Theoretically, pyrrolic N and amino N in NCDs act as active sites to form hydrogen bonds with DMMP. While graphitic N and pyridinic N serve as electron-rich groups, regulating the electron cloud density of N-H and enhancing the hydrogen bond between DMMP and NCDs. (II) Increasing the electrons transfer rate by constructing π-delocalization network. The NCDs prepared from aromatic compounds (oPD) contain a large number of π-conjugated structures, which is obviously different from NCDs from aliphatic compounds [31–33]. Undoubtedly, π-delocalization networks are easily constructed between NCDs and rGO by π-π stacking, effectively adjusting the electron transfer ability of matrix materials. (Ⅲ) Increasing the carrier concentrations by narrowing the band gap of NCDs. The NCDs possess high N content and large π-conjugated structure, leading to narrowing band gap, which is more conducive to electrons transition from the valence band (VB) to conduction band (CB). Notably, the selection of NCDs prepared from oPD with yellow color fluorescent is outstanding innovation, playing a significantly important role in improving the DMMP sensing performances. To further demonstrate the advantages of our proposed approach, single-atom Cu was rationally used to regulate the microstructures of rGO-NCDs. Previously, the single-atom materials mostly act as electron sinks to steer the interfacial charge separation or as active sites for adsorption and activation of molecular oxygen [34,35]. In contrast, the major role of single-atom Cu in this work is used as regulator to modulate electrons distribution of N-H bonds (the main active sites for adsorption of DMMP by hydrogen bond interactions). Fortunately, the above strategy could be simultaneously achieved by introduction of N-enriched NCDs with yellow color and Cu2+ ions onto rGO surface (Fig. 1b). Firstly, the combining UV light irradiation and hydrothermal treatment leads to preparing rGO-NCDs from GO and oPD. Then, rGO-NCDs-Cu was obtained by deposition of Cu2+ ions onto rGO-NCDs via a simple chemical adsorption method.

In this study, the NCDs should possess the obvious and unique properties, including high N content, abundant π-conjugates, yellow fluorescence and small particle size. In this regard, a facile method was proposed to synthesize such NCDs ( Fig. 2a). Initially, the dimer of oPD named as 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) nanoparticles (NPs) were obtained by UV light irradiation of oPD solution [36]. Then, after hydrothermal treatment, these DAP oligomers gather to form the aggregates, and subsequently dehydration/carbonization to form carbon core centers, further forming complete carbon cores. Finally, these connections between carbon cores break to form NCDs. XRD pattern (Fig. 2b) reveals the highly ordered structure inside NCDs, indicating that the carbon core had a small-sized graphene sheet structure [37]. AFM image (Fig. 2c) suggested that NCDs are typical discrete NPs and a height profile, indicating the particle size about 5–6 nm. As shown in the elemental analysis results by the ICP-AES (Fig. 2d), the chemical compositions of NCDs are C 65.68 wt%, N 20.41 wt%, H 4.85 wt% and O 9.06 wt% (calculated), indicating that the N content in the present NCDs is higher than the NCDs prepared by other methods [37,38]. The C1s spectrum of NCDs (Fig. S1) showed four peaks attributed to CC (284.7 eV), C-N (285.8 eV), C-O (286.2 eV) and CO (288.3 eV). The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. S2) indicated three peaks at 531.4, 532.7 and 535.9 eV, attributed to CO, C-O and N = O. The N 1 s spectrum of NCDs (Fig. 2e) showed four peaks at 398.8, 399.9, 401.1 and 402.6 eV, assigned to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, graphitic N and amino N. UV–vis absorption spectrum of DAP dispersion (Fig. S3) displayed the peaks at around 260 nm attributed to π-π* transitions of CC/CN bonds and strong excitation absorption peak at 390 nm assigned to n-π* transitions between conjugated structures of the phenazine ring and -NH2 [39]. After hydrothermal treatment, two absorption peaks of NCDs at 280 nm and 419 nm showed obvious redshift (Fig. 2f), compared to DAP dispersion. Similarly, fluorescent spectrum of NCDs also redshifted from 563 nm for DAP (Fig. S4) to 575 nm (Fig. 2f). These results supported the further polymerization of oligomers during hydrothermal process, increasing the sp2/sp3-hybridized conjugated domain [40]. The observation of fluorescent property of NCDs was further confirmed by the photograph of NCDs dispersion under 365 nm UV light irradiation (Fig. 2g). These observations indicated that yellow fluorescent NCDs were successfully prepared, possessing the unique properties of high N content, large delocalized π-conjugated structure and narrow band gap.

The combining UV light irradiation and hydrothermal treatment also converses GO into rGO, supported by XRD pattern (Fig. S5), FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S6), C 1 s spectrum (Fig. S7), O 1 s spectrum (Fig. S8) and SEM image (Fig. S9). The conversion of GO to rGO is performed by the two steps of UV light reduction and hydrothermal reduction. Firstly, UV light reduction is caused by the production of a highly active electron-hole pair (e--h+) on the GO surface due to photoexcitation, the migration of electrons to oxygen-containing groups, triggering the breaking of chemical bonds [41]. Secondly, at high temperature and pressure, the hydrogen radical (H·) or hydrogen gas (H2) produced by the decomposition of H2O molecules reacts with the oxygen-containing groups in GO, resulting in the reduction of GO to rGO [42]. When GO was introduced into the mixture for the preparation of NCDs, rGO-NCDs could be obtained ( Fig. 3a). The diffraction peak in XRD pattern of rGO-NCDs shifts to larger degree of 26.81º (Fig. S10), compared to rGO (26.07º). Meanwhile, the introduction of NCDs onto rGO also resulted in a series of new bands appearing at 3490, 1212 and 782 cm−1 in FT-IR spectrum, attributed to the N-H groups, C-N groups and aromatic structure [43,44] (Fig. S11). rGO-NCDs showed the N 1 s peak in wide-scan XPS spectrum (Fig. S12), and a new prominent peak appeared at 285.8 eV attributed to C-N bonds in C 1s spectrum (Fig. S13). No obvious change was observed for rGO-NCDs in O 1 s spectrum (Fig. S14), compared to rGO. Notably, N 1s spectrum of rGO-NCDs exhibited the features of pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, graphitic N and amino N at 399.1, 399.8, 401.6 and 402.7 eV (Fig. 3b), and the composition of N-containing species is estimated to be pyridinic N 53.60 at%, pyrrolic N 29.23 at%, graphitic N 7.75 at% and amino N 9.43 at% (Fig. 3c). The SEM image of rGO-NCDs (Fig. 3d) suggested typical wrinkled layered structures attributed to that NCDs were intercalated into rGO nanosheets through electrostatic interactions and π-π interaction.

The energy level structure had a great impact on electrons transfer efficiency between sensing materials and DMMP molecules. For the semiconductor properties, rGO is a typical p-type semiconductor (Mott-Schottky plot, Fig. S15) with band gap of 3.89 eV (UV-Vis spectrum, Fig. S16). While NCDs are typical p-type semiconductors with band gap of 2.56 eV (Fig. S17, S18). As well known that the work functions of NCDs and rGO are 3.09 eV [45] and 4.41 eV [46]. When NCDs and rGO contracted each other, the electrons will transfer from NCDs to rGO, resulting in the band bending of energy bands for them. Meantime, a build-in electric field (BEF) between the NCDs and rGO was formed, which provides the driving force for preventing electron directed transfer [47]. Finally, an equilibrium of electron transfer process is established between NCDs and rGO, and Fermi levels achieves equilibrium [48], leading to forming p-p heterojunction between their interface (Fig. 3e).

Theoretically, the introduction of single-atom Cu onto rGO-NCDs not only acts as modifier to effectively modulate the electron structure, but also provides additional active sites for adsorption of DMMP. Therefore, owing to containing electron-rich N atoms, NCDs could serve as anchoring sites for stabilizing single-atom Cu, and rGO-NCDs-Cu was rationally prepared (Fig. 3f). As shown in Fig. 3g, FT-IR spectrum of rGO-NCDs-Cu showed C-N stretching vibration shifted from 1212 cm−1 (rGO-NCDs) to 1216 cm−1 and a new band appearing at 782 cm−1 attributed to Cu-N bonds [49]. This result is also supported by wide-scan XPS spectrum (Fig. S19) and N 1 s spectrum [50] (Fig. S20). Additionally, there are no obvious difference between rGO-NCDs and rGO-NCDs-Cu for XRD pattern (Fig. S21), C 1 s spectrum (Fig. S22), O 1 s spectrum (Fig. S23). The TEM image of rGO-NCDs-Cu (Fig. S24) reveals thin nanosheets with size of several micrometers evenly decorated with numerous NPs of several nanometers (NCDs). High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image (Fig. 3h) indicated that particle size of NCDs is about 5 nm, consistent with the results of AFM. No observation of Cu-containing species in TEM image may be attributed to the absence of solid products (CuO, Cu2O) or low Cu content. The successful preparation of rGO-NCDs-Cu was further demonstrated by the change of zeta potentials for various samples (Fig. S25) and the photographs of aqueous solution containing different samples (Fig. S26).

The chemical structure of Cu2+ ions were examined detailed. As shown in Cu 2p spectrum of rGO-NCDs-Cu ( Fig. 4a), two distinctive peaks at 935.1 and 954.9 eV were assigned to Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 for Cu2+ [51], while another two peaks at 932.8 and 952.7 eV were attributed to Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 for Cu+ [52]. Additionally, the statellite peaks are also comprised of peaks attributed to both Cu2+ and Cu+. The possible reason for the formation of Cu+ ions in rGO-NCDs-Cu could be dominantly derived from Cu-N sites. The charges (electrons) transferred from N atoms contribute to the reduction of Cu2+ partly into Cu+ due to the low content of Cu element in hybrids [22]. According to Cu 2p peak, the percentages of Cu2+ and Cu+ are about 54 % and 46 % (Fig. 4b). Notably, the Cu+ content (46 %) was significantly higher than Cu+ content (32 %) in previous work [43], suggesting that the Cu-N coordination leaded to more Cu2+ reduced to Cu+. The Cu K-edge X-ray absorption fine structure spectroscopy (XAFS) spectra were obtained to determine the chemical state and coordination environment of Cu atoms in rGO-NCDs-Cu. The fitting quantitative χ(R) space and Fourier-transform (FT) k2-weighted function χ(k) spectra were also performed to investigate local atomic structure, further deriving the coordination numbers of Cu element. Other referenced samples including standard Cu foil, CuO, Cu2O and Cu(II) phthalocyanine (CuPc) were also involved. The X-ray absorption fine structure spectroscopy (XANES) spectra (Fig. 4c) showed that the Cu-K absorption edge energy was located between those of Cu2O and CuO, indicating that the valence for Cu atom in rGO-NCDs-Cu was between + 1 and + 2 [53,54] (Fig. 4d), in agreement with the XPS analysis. The shoulder feature appeared at 8979 eV originates from ligand-to-hybrids charge transfer. The mean chemical valence of Cu element in rGO-NCDs-Cu is + 1.7 (Fig. 4e). In Fig. 4f, the K-edge k3 χ spectrum displayed a main peak at 1.49 Å, corresponded to the Cu-N first shell [55]. However, unlike the standard Cu-N peak of CuPc, rGO-NCDs-Cu displayed a wide peak at this position, indicating that Cu atoms coordinated with N and other atoms. According to XPS full spectrum of rGO-NCDs-Cu, the sample still contains less Cl element, and the corresponding reference show that the K-edge k3 χ spectrum of Cu-Cl peak at ∼1.4 Å [56]. We speculate that Cu atoms in rGO-NCDs-Cu also form a coordination with Cl atom. The peaks at 2.20 Å and 1.53 Å assigned to the Cu-Cu and Cu-O coordination are not observed, suggesting that Cu atoms were separated from each other and coordinated with N atoms and Cl atoms [57]. Furthermore, the quantitative extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS) fitting results showed that the average coordination number of Cu atoms in rGO-NCDs-Cu is close to 4 (Fig. 4g and Table S1). As a standard 4-coordination structure, CuPc has important reference for the analysis of Cu atoms in rGO-NCDs-Cu. The absorption threshold position of rGO-NCDs-Cu shifted to a lower energy than that of CuPc, which indicates that the asymmetric coordination structure of rGO-NCDs-Cu resulted in a lower valence state (Fig. 4c). Usually, the four coordinate structure of single-atom Cu could be presented with tetrahedron or square configurations. In this work, single-atom Cu was prepared by immersion method, resulting in that single-atom Cu can not be able to enter the NCDs. Owing to that it is hard to form the defect surrounded by four coplanar N atoms, it is impossible to construct a square structure. Thus, single-atom Cu only could be presented with tetrahedron structure, which was further confirmed by the observation of Cu-Cl in Fig. 4f. Based on above analysis, Cu atomic coordination diagram is identified as the “Cu-N2Cl2” moiety (inset of Fig. 4g and Fig. S27).

The scheme of the home-made gas sensing measurement system is shown in Fig. S28. The pristine rGO sensor showed a response value of 0.94 % to 340 ppb DMMP ( Fig. 5a), which is attributed to the poor adsorption capacity of rGO towards DMMP by the van der Waals force. The modification of rGO with Cu2+ ions revealed the response value of 5.0 % to 340 ppb (Fig. 5b), because Cu2+ can act as active sites to form weak coordination with DMMP. Notably, rGO-NCDs sensor showed the enhanced response value of 6.4 % to 340 ppb DMMP (Fig. 5c), which is 5.8-fold higher than that of rGO. This result could be attributed to N-enriched NCDs, possessing a large number of N-H groups as active sites. Interestingly, the simultaneous introduction of NCDs and Cu2+ induced that rGO-NCDs-Cu sensor had the greatest response value of 13.95 % to 340 ppb DMMP (Fig. 5d), which is 13.8 times higher than that of rGO sensor (0.94 %) and 1.2 times higher than that of rGO-NCDs (6.41 %) (Fig. 5e). The introduction of Cu2+ allowed more electrons passing through the delocalized π-conjugated network of rGO-NCDs to Cu sites, which facilitates charge transfer between rGO-NCDs layers, resulting in more effective separation of electron/hole pairs and optimizing charge distribution. Additionally, Fig. S29 shows the response/recovery time of these rGO-based DMMP sensors toward 340 ppb DMMP. The response time/recovery time for rGO (51 s/89 s) are shorter than that of rGO-Cu (74 s/158 s), rGO-NCDs (76 s/190 s) and rGO-NCDs-Cu (77 s/198 s). After the introduction of single-atom Cu and NCDs onto rGO nanosheets, the response/recovery time became longer, which was attributed to the construction of multiple active sites on rGO surface. The aforementioned findings showed that simultaneously introducing single-atom Cu and NCDs onto rGO nanosheets greatly increased the sensitivity of DMMP sensors. This is advantageous for the fabrication of high-performance room-temperature DMMP sensors. By fabricating six rGO-NCDs-Cu sensors in parallel, variation of initial resistances and DMMP response exhibit minor fluctuations (Fig. S30), indicating good reproducibility and good stability.

In order to examine the effect of CDs structure on gas sensitivity, two controlled carbon dots were used to modify rGO for DMMP sensing. Firstly, NCDs with yellow fluorescence property containing few π-conjugated structures were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of CA and urea, followed by modifying rGO by hydrothermal method, designated as rGO-NCDs (CA), and they showed a response value of 4.57 % to 340 ppb DMMP (Figs. 5f, 5h), which is lower than that of rGO-NCDs (6.41 %), indicating the π-conjugated structures contributed to the improvement of DMMP sensing performances. Secondly, in order to prove that N-H groups can form hydrogen bonds and effectively improve the DMMP sensitive performances, CDs with yellow fluorescent property without N elements were prepared from AA, followed by modifying rGO, designated as rGO-CDs (AA), which exhibited low response value of 3.19 % to 340 ppb DMMP (Figs. 5g, 5h). This result indicated that the N-H groups formed on the surface of sensitive material can effectively adsorb DMMP molecules. It is not difficult to see that the construction of hydrogen bond bridges between the sensitive material and DMMP, the regulation of electron distribution of sensing materials, and the improvement of electron migration rate are important factors to improve DMMP sensitive property.

The effect of Cu2+ concentrations on DMMP sensing characteristics showed that 50 mM is the optimal concentration for preparation of sensing materials (Fig. S31). The transient resistance changes of rGO-NCDs-Cu sensor to DMMP with concentrations of 34–340 ppb were examined ( Fig. 6a). As the DMMP concentrations increased from 34 to 340 ppb, the corresponding response value increased from 3.12 % to 13.95 %. The limit of detection (LOD) for rGO-NCDs-Cu sensor is as low as 34 ppb. The DMMP concentrations as a function of response values reveals approximately linear relationship (Fig. 6b). The repeatability of rGO-NCDs-Cu sensor for 340 ppb DMMP (Fig. 6c) revealed the constant response values, reversible relationship and stable characteristics during each cyclic exposure. The selectivity of rGO-NCDs-Cu sensor toward DMMP was further investigated by exposing to 340 ppb DMMP and 100 ppm other analytes (methanol, ethanol, NH3 and NO2), demonstrating excellent selectivity toward DMMP (Fig. 6d). Fig. S32a-d presented dynamic resistance transition of rGO-NCDs-Cu sensor to 340 ppb DMMP under 0 % RH, 25 % RH, 50 % RH and 75 % RH, presenting the response values of 13.95 %, 8.75 %, 5.28 % and 3.91 % (Fig. 6e). The reason for this result was that H2O molecules occupy the active sites on sensing materials, preventing DMMP molecules from adhering to the material.

Notably, the rGO-NCDs-Cu sensor shows relatively high noise during the sensing processes. The relatively high noise for DMMP sensor may be due to uneven distribution of NCDs growth on GO (maybe partly reduced GO) in the external light radiation step. The inhomogeneity of the UV light source will cause the energy distribution in the reaction region to be inconsistent, which will affect the generation and distribution of NCDs. Resistive-type gas sensors use these carbon film materials as electron carriers. When the current flows, the conductivity of the resistor will change and the current will change, resulting in a similar flash arc [58]. Owing to that sensing response in this work is reliable, this high noise does not mean the poor stability of sensing materials and sensors.

According to the above results, a possible DMMP sensing mechanism was proposed (Fig. 6f). (I) When rGO-NCDs-Cu sensor was exposed to air, the sensor had a higher concentration of holes (charger carrier) in air, resulting in lower resistance. (II) After exposure to DMMP, DMMP molecules will be adsorbed by rGO-NCDs-Cu. Because DMMP was a strong electron donor, electrons will pass from DMMP to rGO-NCDs-Cu. A reduction in holes concentration will occur, companying with increasing resistance [59,60]. (III) After exposure to air again, the desorption of DMMP from rGO-NCDs-Cu resulted in the recovering the resistance back to initial values. Fig. 6g showed the comparison of rGO-NCDs-Cu sensor with room-temperature DMMP sensors in publications [18,19,43,61–67]. Notably, the present rGO-NCDs-Cu sensor exhibited low LOD of 34 ppb, which is lower than most of room-temperature DMMP sensors.

The rGO-NCDs-Cu-Ⅰ obtained by UV light irradiation without hydrothermal treatment (Fig. S33a) exhibits response value (12.61 %) similar with rGO-NCD-Cu (13.95 %) (Fig. S33b). However, this sensor shows strong noise and poor response stability. The rGO-NCDs-Cu-Ⅱ prepared by the method without UV light irradiation (Fig. S34a) shows the low response value of 3.75 % to 340 ppb DMMP (Fig. S34b). In addition, rGO-NCDs-Cu-Ⅲ (Fig. S35a) obtained by assemble of NCDs (not in situ deposition from oPD) exhibits the low response value of 5.99 % and poor stability of resistance (Fig. S35b). Compared to above sensors, the rGO-NCD-Cu based sensor exhibits a higher response and fastest response to recovery, indicating that hydrothermal treatment, UV light irradiation, in situ assembly are important factors for preparation of rGO-NCDs-Cu with enhanced DMMP sensing performances.

In addition, the sensor sensing response reliability is also discussed. Firstly, there are undeniably that the rGO-NCDs-Cu sensor exhibits relatively low response value (13.95 % to 340 ppb DMMP), compared to most metal oxides-based gas sensors. However, this sensor has demonstrated good stability, good repeatability, and high selectivity in DMMP tests, indicating the high measurement accuracy, not the unreliable data. In the subsequent sensor commercialization process, a suitable circuit system can be adapted to increase the signal output for rGO-NCDs-Cu sensor. Secondly, the signal-to-noise ratio is a standard for determining whether noise affects sensing performance [58]. According to International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) regulation, the signal must be at least three times larger in magnitude than the noise in order to be considered a valid signal (SNR >3) [68]. In this work, the SNR of the rGO-NCDs-Cu sensor is 32.04–340 ppb DMMP and 4.23 for the lowest detectable concentration of 34 ppb DMMP. Therefore, the data we present in the manuscript are valid, reliable signal data.

To clearly illustrate innovations in comparison with the data reported in previously reported work [43], Table S2 compares the structural changes of N-CNPs and NCDs as following three aspects (particle size, carbon core structure and band gap). Table S3 shows the comparison in the N and Cu content of rGO-CNPs-Cu2+ (GNC) and rGO-NCDs-Cu hybrids. Moreover, Fig. S36 shows the schematic diagram of structural characteristics of rGO-NCNPs-Cu2+ and rGO-NCDs-Cu, and the differences between the two materials and their effects on gas-sensitive properties are discussed in detail.

Generally, the DMMP sensing mechanism of rGO-NCDs-Cu sensors is depended on the charges transfer between the sensing materials and DMMP molecules, leading to adjusting the holes concentration and changing the sensor resistance. Our previous publication has shown that rGO, N-CNPs and Cu2+ ions are the active sites for the adsorption of DMMP through Van der Waals, hydrogen bonding interactions and coordination effect [43]. Among them, the hydrogen bonding interaction is the most important factor for these room-temperature DMMP sensors. Theoretically, many factors influence the DMMP sensing performances, including the charge carrier density, pathway of charge carrier, and interaction between the DMMP molecules and sensing materials. Here, the reason for significant improvement of DMMP sensing characteristics for rGO-NCDs-Cu sensor has been examined, and the possible mechanism can be concluded as follows.

(1) Strengthening the hydrogen bonding interactions. Hydrogen bonding interactions are normally affected by the number of hydrogen bonds and the strength of hydrogen bonds. As a CDs with higher N content, NCDs contained a large number of N-containing functional groups (graphitic N, pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and amino N), where pyrrolic N and amino N contribute to increasing the number of hydrogen bonds and graphitic N and pyridinic N strongly strength hydrogen bonds interactions.

(2) Construction of heterojunctions by rGO and NCDs with narrow band gap. There are two important aspects to be considered for interface properties of heterojunctions. One is the lattice match between the two components, which is the prerequisite for fabricating heterojunctions. The other one is the suitable CB and/or VB potentials, which are considered to be the prime and fundamental requirement for charge transfer and separation at the heterojunction interfaces [69]. NCDs exhibited reserved graphitic domains (sp2 domains) and had similar graphitic in-plane lattice spacing, easily forms heterojunctions and a potential barrier is established at the interface of rGO and NCDs. The formation of potential barrier accelerates the electron migration rate at the interface, causing drastically variations of resistance of the sensor. Moreover, the NCDs possess high N content and large π-conjugated structure, leading to narrowing band gap, which is more conducive to electron transition from the VB to CB.

(3) Fabrication of interface structure with π-conjugated structure. The π-π stacking is a well-recognized intermolecular interaction, which is responsible for the construction of electron hopping channels in numerous composite interfaces [70]. The π-π stacking between NCDs and rGO easily constructs a vast π delocalization network, which effectively modifies the electron transfer capacity, and alters the rate of electron migration between interfaces led to hole density on the surface of the sensitive material changes significantly in a short time, making the sensor have faster response time and high sensitivity.

(4) Coupling single-atom Cu. Single-atom Cu mainly regulates DMMP sensing performance by the following two aspects. Firstly, according to the valence bond theory, the empty orbital of Cu2+ ions (3d9) and the lone pair electrons in the N atoms of NCDs enable to form Cu-N coordination bonds. XPS results and XANES results indicate that the formation of Cu-N coordination structure leads to the electron transfer from N atoms to Cu atoms, which can significantly enhance the electronic deficiency of H atom in NCDs, subsequently enhancing the formation of hydrogen bonds between DMMP and NCDs. Secondly, single-atom Cu bridges serve as electron channels to promote electron/hole pair separation and interlayer charge transport [71].

In summary, the room-temperature ppb-level DMMP sensors were constructed using rGO-NCDs-Cu as sensing materials. The rGO-NCDs-Cu sensor shows a higher response value of 13.95 % with excellent repeatability and outstanding selectivity. More importantly, the rGO-NCDs-Cu sensor shows short response time and recovery time (77 s and 198 s) and low limit of detection (34 ppb), making it suitable for real-time low concentration DMMP detection in practical application. These improved DMMP sensing properties can be attributed to introduction regulation of hydrogen bonding interactions and semiconductor properties by NCDs with N-enriched structures, narrowed band gap and π-conjugated structure, the regulation of electron structure by single-atom Cu. This study provides a novel strategy for designing and developing highly sensitive and high selective room-temperature gas sensors.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yunpeng Xing received his B.S. degree from the College of chemistry, Jilin Normal University, China in 2020. Currently, he is a doctoral student in College of Electronics Science and Engineering from Jilin University. His research focuses on graphene-based DMMP sensors.

Liang Zhao received his B.S. degree from the Electronics Science and Engineering, Jilin University, China in 2021. Currently, he is a doctoral student in College of Electronics Science and Engineering from Jilin University. His research focuses on the electronic structure regulation of Co-based materials and gas sensing applications.

Hongda Zhang received his B.S. degree from the College of Materials Science and Engineering, Harbin University of Science and Technology, China in 2019. He received his M.S. degree in College of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering from Harbin University of Science and Technology in 2022. Currently, he is a doctoral student in College of Electronics Science and Engineering from Jilin University. His research interests focus on metal oxide-based gas sensors.

Chengchao Yu received his B.S. degree from the Electronics Science and Engineering, Jilin University, China in 2022. Currently, he is a master student in College of Electronics Science and Engineering from Jilin University. His research focuses on NiO-based gas sensors.

Sihao Zhi received his B.S. degree from the Electronics Science and Engineering, Jilin University, China in 2021. Currently, he is a master student in College of Electronics Science and Engineering from Jilin University. His research focuses on metal oxide-based DMMP sensors.

Teng Fei received the B.S. degree in chemical engineering and technology and the Ph.D. degree in polymer chemistry and physics from Jilin University, China, in 2005 and 2010, respectively. He is currently a Professor with the College of Electronics Science and Engineering, Jilin University. His research interests include sensing functional materials and devices.

Sen Liu received the Ph.D. degree in inorganic chemistry from Jilin University in 2010. He is currently a Professor with the College of Electronics Science and Engineering, Jilin University. His current research is focused on electronic structure regulation of semiconductors for chemical sensors.

Haiyan Zhang received the Ph.D. degree in inorganic chemistry from Jilin University in 2013. She is currently a senior engineer with the School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jilin University. Her current research is focused on design of functional materials and their applications.

Tong Zhang completed her MS degree in semiconductor materials in 1992 and her Ph.D. in the field of microelectronics and solid-state electronics in 2001 from Jilin University. She was appointed as a full-time professor at the College of Electronics Science and Engineering, Jilin University in 2001. Her research interests focus on design of sensing functional materials and sensors research on gas sensor, humidity sensor, electrochemical sensor, flexible wearable pressure sensor and so on.
